query: clinical manifestations of selective IgA deficiency

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: With its lack of direct signs and symptoms, IgA deficiency can be overshadowed by other ailments. The presence of clinical acumen, supplemented by suspicion, might be the only and best diagnostic weapon.

retrieved_docs.title: Antibody Deficiency Disorder (Archived) -- Etiology -- Selective Ig Immunodeficiencies (IgA Selective Deficiency)
retrieved_docs.content: Normally, antibodies switch from IgM to other classes, including IgG and IgA. Sometimes there is malfunctioning in heavy-chain gene switching and causes this problem. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of selective IgA deficiency is often is one of exclusion. The following conditions are examples of what must be excluded while making a diagnosis of IgA deficiency:

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Some autoimmune diseases can have manifestations that can cloud or cover IgA deficiency necessitating an additional workup. Certain conditions like ataxia-telangiectasia can be problematic based on their multiple immunoglobulin deficiencies (IgA, IgG, and IgE are all decreased, unlike in IgA deficiency, where the IgG is normal). Patients with Hyper-IgM syndrome will present with decreased IgA as part of a hypergammaglobulinemia where only the IgM is elevated. These patients present with severe infections and hyperviscosity (due to the increased IgM) and are inherited in either an X-linked or autosomal recessive format. [23] Selective IgA deficiency should be a possibility in every workup of an autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiologies of immunoglobulin A deficiency. Review the evaluation that should be considered in patients with immunoglobulin A deficiency. Describe the management of immunoglobulin A deficiency. Identify interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and outcomes in patients with immunoglobulin A deficiency. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The European Society for immunodeficiencies (ESID) new criteria for diagnosing selective IgA deficiency include the following constellation: Undetectable serum IgA levels (below 0.07 g/l, measured by nephelometry), although normal serum levels of IgG and IgM (evaluated by nephelometry at least twice) Enhanced susceptibility to infections, especially respiratory Diagnosis at an age older than four years (reportedly the age at which nephelometry can measure IgA with precision). Concomitant autoimmune diseases or a family member with the disease Excluded secondary causes of hypogammaglobulinemia Normal antibody (IgG) response to all vaccinations Exclusion of T-cell defect

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The most common complications are associated with respiratory infections and malignancies. Following complications are associated with IgA deficiency:

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Immunoglobulin A (IgA) deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency characterized by a decreased amount of serum IgA and a concomitant lack of secretory IgA. Other immunoglobulin levels are normal. By the International Consensus definition, the diagnosis is established in individuals older than four years whose blood levels of IgA are below 7 mg/dl but have normal levels of IgG and IgM. (IgE is  normal too.) These individuals have a normal antibody response to vaccines. Nevertheless, IgA deficiency should be excluded from other causes of hypogammaglobulinemia and defects in T lymphocytes. Patients who suffer from selective IgA deficiency may have clinical symptoms, but most are largely asymptomatic. [1] [2] IgA deficiency is often associated with normal CD4 and CD8 T cells, with normal B lymphocytes in the systemic circulation. Some patients, though,  may harbor anti-IgA autoantibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis, overall, is typically good. Some patients with IgA deficiency may resolve spontaneously. For others, the repeated bouts of infection and/or inflammation, whether compounded by cancer or autoimmune disease, lead to varying rates of decline in health and a cause for trepidation.

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most IgA patients remain asymptomatic their whole life. Coincidentally, they are often diagnosed during routine laboratory screening. However, some patients present with different complaints and clinical phenotypes, mainly with recurrent sinopulmonary infection, allergies, autoimmune diseases, gastrointestinal disorders, malignancies, and other severe complications. [1] The most common manifestations associated with selective IgA deficiency are the recurrent pulmonary infections caused by extracellular encapsulated bacteria such as Streptococcus pneumoniae and  Haemophilus influenzae . More severe symptoms occur when selective IgA deficiency is combined with IgG2 and IgG3 subclass deficiency. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: A variety of anti-rheumatic (sulphasalazine, gold), antiepileptic drugs (carbamazepine, valproate), and ACE inhibitors have the potential to lower the IgA levels (See 'Etiology').

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and lymphoma can have a decreased level of IgA but at the more advanced stages of the disease and typically as part of a pan-hypogammaglobulinemic format. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary function tests are recommended to follow patients with IgA deficiency. Patients with recurrent diarrhea should undergo jejunal biopsy, which may show blunting of the villi. The histopathology may reveal the absence of IgA secreting cells and the presence of IgM secretory cells in the lamina propria.

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Single gene primary immunodeficiencies like X-linked hyper IgM syndrome could be easily differentiated from selective IgA deficiency (For hyper-IgM syndrome, see 'History and Physical'). Low levels of IgA can be seen with deficiencies of one or more IgG subclasses. A normal response is seen to some vaccinations. Infections by encapsulated bacteria ( Hemophilus influenza and Streptococcus pneumoniae ) are seen here in recurrent sinopulmonary infections as they are in IgA deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The worldwide prevalence of selective IgA deficiency depends on the ethnic background; it is most prevalent in White-race individuals. The incidence varies from 1:143 to 1:965 in different regions, with equal distribution between the genders. [11] However, the real rate could be higher due to the absence of routine screening programs for immunodeficiencies and because many patients are asymptomatic. It is estimated that the prevalence of selective IgA deficiency is 38-fold higher in first-degree relatives of the patients with the disorder than unrelated donors, and the chance to inherit the disease in the family is about 20%. [1] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

The laboratory test PBI is related to اضطراب الدم. نقص غلوبيولين الدم (blood‑protein deficiency) is a child condition of اضطراب الدم and serves as a parent condition for immunity disorders, highlighting the link between global immune dysfunction and specific globulin deficiencies. كثرة الخلايا البدينة (eosinophilia) is a parent category of اضطراب الدم. Diseases and injuries are grouped under اضطراب الدم, and سرطان المعدة is a specific condition within سرطان المعدة. The overlapping clinical presentations across these interconnected disorders are emphasized by the inclusion of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions as related and possibly synonymous entities.

Blood disorders are situated within the overarching category of diseases and injuries, which falls under the broader classification of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within the blood‑disorder hierarchy, excess of round cells (كثرة الخلايا البدينة) serves as a parent category, while blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم) is a child condition, illustrating a continuum from cellular abnormalities to protein deficiencies. The diagnostic test PBI is associated with blood disorders and provides a tool for clinical evaluation. Malaise and fatigue are classified as signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions and are expressed in Arabic medical terminology as الشعور بالضعف and النوام, with الضعف العصبي as an alternative mapping. Immunity disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities.

This reflects the multifactorial nature of white‑cell depletion, which can arise from nutritional protein intolerance, viral infection, or underlying anemic processes. The sign‑symptom complex is also clinically associated with chest radiography (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر) and with gastritis and duodenitis. Gastritis and duodenitis themselves have multiple clinical associations: they are linked to anemia, to bloody stool, and to a positive HIV test, highlighting how gastrointestinal inflammation can coexist with hematologic abnormalities and immunodeficiency. Moreover, gastritis and duodenitis give rise to a child condition—erosive gastritis (الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ)—illustrating a hierarchical relationship within gastrointestinal disease. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 367
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is classified as a specific type of اضطراب الدم (blood disorder), and اضطراب الدم also encompasses نقص غلوبيولين الدم (immunoglobulin deficiency) as one of its sub‑conditions. نقص غلوبيولين الدم, in turn, serves as a parent category for broader immunity disorders, linking deficiencies in blood proteins to systemic immune dysfunction. Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, which are situated under the umbrella of داء الكولاجين‑الوعائي (vascular collagen disease), represent a major connective‑tissue and autoimmune condition that shares pathophysiological overlap with other blood‑related abnormalities. Multiple glucocorticoid formulations—methylprednisolon acetát, methylprednisolone aceponide, methylprednisolone sodium phosphate, and methylprednisolone sodium succinate—are indicated for the treatment of both اضطراب الدم and systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, reflecting their broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects across hematologic and autoimmune diseases. Additional agents employed in systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders include triamcinolonacetonid (tri‑amcinolone acetonide), dexamethasonisonikotinát (a dexamethasone derivative), and hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine), each contributing to disease control through distinct mechanisms of immune modulation. Conversely, certain substances are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders; specifically, trioxsalen and zlato should be avoided because of their potential to exacerbate the underlying autoimmune pathology. Understanding the hierarchical relationships among blood disorders, immunoglobulin deficiencies, and connective‑tissue autoimmune diseases, as well as the shared and distinct therapeutic options and contraindications, is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and individualized treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1799
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis is a gastrointestinal inflammatory condition that is classified as a specific manifestation within the broader category of الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis). It can give rise to or be related to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, indicating a more severe or erosive form of gastric inflammation. Clinically, gastritis and duodenitis has been associated with تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى, suggesting the presence of bloody stool and possible lower gastrointestinal bleeding, and with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي, indicating that patients who are HIV‑positive may be at increased risk for or present with this condition. Recognizing these relationships assists clinicians in evaluating disease severity, considering underlying immunocompromise, and selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.
